note_data.title: Financial accounting fundamentals
keyPoints: **Accounting equation**: Assets = Capital + Liabilities must always balance after every transaction
**Double entry** principle states that every transaction affects two accounts with equal and opposite entries
**Debit** entries record increases in assets and expenses, decreases in liabilities, capital and income
**Credit** entries record increases in liabilities, capital and income, decreases in assets and expenses
**Books of prime entry** are the first books where transactions are recorded before posting to the ledger
**Trial balance** is a list of all ledger account balances to check the arithmetical accuracy of double entry
**Sales day book** records all credit sales transactions chronologically
**Purchases day book** records all credit purchases transactions chronologically
**Cash book** records all cash and bank transactions and forms part of the double entry system
**General journal** records transactions that cannot be recorded in other books of prime entry

definitions.definition: A system of bookkeeping where every transaction is recorded twice with equal debits and credits
A list of all ledger account balances at a specific date to test the arithmetical accuracy of the books
Books where transactions are first recorded before being posted to the ledger accounts
Assets equals Capital plus Liabilities - the fundamental equation that must always balance
The process of transferring entries from books of prime entry to the relevant ledger accounts

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Set out accounts in T-account format with proper headings and dates
Show all workings clearly and label your final answer

modelAnswer.question: Explain the purpose of preparing a trial balance. (3 marks)
modelAnswer.answer: A trial balance is prepared to test the arithmetical accuracy of the double entry bookkeeping system. It ensures that total debits equal total credits, which proves that the double entry principle has been correctly applied. It also provides a summary of all ledger account balances which can be used as a basis for preparing final accounts.
markBreakdown: Mark 2: To ensure debits equal credits/double entry correctly applied
Mark 3: To provide summary of balances for final accounts preparation

commonMistakes: Never say trial balance 'proves accounts are correct' — it only tests arithmetical accuracy, not completeness or accuracy of transactions
Students often forget that cash book is both a book of prime entry AND part of the ledger system
Never debit liabilities or credit assets when they increase — this reverses the double entry rules
Students confuse sales day book with sales account — day book is just a list, not part of double entry
Never say 'balance sheet equation' — always use 'accounting equation'
examinerTips: Trial balance questions appear in almost every Paper 1 and Paper 2
Always check that your trial balance totals are equal — unequal totals lose marks
Learn the DEAD CLIC rule: Debits - Expenses, Assets, Drawings; Credits - Liabilities, Income, Capital
Books of prime entry are frequently tested — know the purpose of each book
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: T-account format showing debit side (left) and credit side (right) with account name at the top and proper date, details, and amount columns
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P1 Q1 (6 marks) — they asked students to prepare a trial balance from given account balances and identify two errors that would cause it not to balance
This topic was tested in O/N 2022 P2 Q4 (8 marks) — they required candidates to prepare a trading account and profit and loss account from incomplete records, calculating missing figures
This topic was tested in M/J 2022 P1 Q3 (4 marks) — they asked students to explain the difference between capital and revenue expenditure with two examples of each
This topic was tested in O/N 2023 P2 Q2 (10 marks) — they required preparation of a balance sheet from given information, including proper classification of current and non-current assets

workedExample.question: From the following information, prepare a trial balance for ABC Trading as at 31 December 2023: Cash $2,000, Bank $15,000, Debtors $8,000, Stock $12,000, Equipment $25,000, Creditors $6,000, Bank loan $20,000, Capital $36,000. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify debit balances (assets and expenses): Cash $2,000, Bank $15,000, Debtors $8,000, Stock $12,000, Equipment $25,000
Step 2: Identify credit balances (liabilities, capital, and income): Creditors $6,000, Bank loan $20,000, Capital $36,000
Step 3: List all debit balances in the debit column, totaling $62,000
Step 4: List all credit balances in the credit column, totaling $62,000
Step 5: Verify that debit total equals credit total to confirm trial balance accuracy
workedExample.answer:
ABC Trading Trial Balance as at 31 December 2023
Dr side: Cash $2,000, Bank $15,000, Debtors $8,000, Stock $12,000, Equipment $25,000, Total $62,000
Cr side: Creditors $6,000, Bank loan $20,000, Capital $36,000, Total $62,000
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct heading, 1 mark for each correctly classified item (5 marks total), 1 mark for balanced totals

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused revenue expenditure with capital expenditure' — always remember that revenue expenditure benefits current period only while capital expenditure provides benefits for multiple years

Examiner Report 2022: 'Candidates frequently placed items on wrong side of trial balance' — always remember that assets and expenses are debit balances, while liabilities, capital and income are credit balances

Examiner Report 2023: 'Students often forgot to include proper headings and dates in financial statements' — always include business name, statement type, and period/date in your heading for full marks
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw the Accounting Equation triangle showing Assets at the top, with Capital and Liabilities at the bottom two corners. Include the formula Assets = Capital + Liabilities in the center. Add arrows showing how increases/decreases in one element affect the others. Label each section with examples: Assets (Cash, Stock, Equipment), Capital (Owner's investment, Retained profits), Liabilities (Creditors, Loans). This diagram is essential for understanding double-entry principles and balance sheet preparation.
note_data.quickRecap: Financial accounting follows the fundamental equation: Assets = Capital + Liabilities. All transactions are recorded using double-entry bookkeeping (every debit has a corresponding credit). The trial balance proves arithmetical accuracy with total debits equaling total credits. Financial statements (Trading Account, Profit & Loss Account, Balance Sheet) are prepared from the trial balance to show business performance and financial position.
